name: Rust
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Rust as a competency should be able to explain Rust’s goals (memory safety, zero-cost abstractions), use correct Rust editions, write idiomatic functions and modules, declare variables with proper mutability and scope, use primitive and composite types, apply control flow constructs (`if`, `loop`, `for`), perform pattern matching with `match` and `if let`, define and use `struct` and `enum` types, add methods via `impl`, manage visibility with `pub`, read and resolve compiler errors, and understand stack vs heap allocation and ownership, borrowing, and lifetimes at a conceptual level. They should use references and mutable references, distinguish `Copy` types, resolve borrow checker issues, use lifetime elision, and avoid `unsafe` code. They can design custom types, derive and use standard traits (`Debug`, `Clone`, `PartialEq`), write generic functions and types, use trait bounds, core generics (`Option`, `Result`, `Box`), and trait objects. They handle errors with `Result` and `Option`, propagate errors with `?`, define simple error types, avoid overusing `unwrap`/`panic!`, and use utility methods on `Result`/`Option`. They select and use standard collections (`Vec`, `HashMap`, `HashSet`), manage ownership in collections, work with strings (`String`, `&str`), use iterators and adapters (`map`, `filter`, `collect`), and understand iterator consumption. They spawn threads with `std::thread`, use `Arc`, `Mutex`, channels, and basic synchronization, understand `Send`/`Sync`, and avoid deadlocks. They organize code into modules and crates, manage visibility, use `use` and paths, maintain `Cargo.toml`, and understand workspaces. They write `async fn`, use `.await`, leverage an async runtime (e.g., Tokio), combine async tasks, and handle async errors. They write and run unit and integration tests, use assertions, maintain code quality with rustfmt and Clippy, and write documentation tests. They are aware of performance basics, minimize unnecessary allocations, use efficient collection operations, and build release binaries. They use Cargo for dependency management, evaluate third-party crates, use Serde for serialization, and adapt to project ecosystem choices. They manage toolchains with rustup, use Cargo for build/test/doc, configure basic Cargo settings, use IDE tools (rust-analyzer), and keep tooling updated. They debug with println/logging, use backtraces, basic debuggers, add logging, perform simple benchmarking, and analyze error logs. They document APIs with doc comments, write runnable examples, follow team conventions, participate in code reviews, use version control, and contribute to project documentation.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Rust Language Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understands Rust’s core goals (memory safety without GC, zero-cost abstractions, explicit control) and can explain how this shapes language design and trade-offs
- Uses Rust editions (2018/2021+) correctly and understands that code behavior and features depend on the selected edition
- Writes idiomatic Rust functions and modules using expressions, statements, blocks, and return values without relying on unnecessary `return` or `mut`
- Declares and uses variables with correct mutability (`let`, `let mut`) and shadowing, and understands scope and lifetime of variables at a basic level
- Uses primitive types (`bool`, integer and floating types, `char`, `&str`, `String`) and composite types (tuples, arrays, slices) correctly in function signatures and implementations
- Applies core control-flow constructs (`if`, `loop`, `while`, `for`) and understands when to choose each based on the problem
- Uses pattern matching (`match`, `if let`, `while let`) for control flow and deconstruction of enums, tuples, and structs in an idiomatic way
- Defines and uses `struct` and `enum` types to model domain data and state, including tuple structs and unit-like structs
- Adds inherent methods and associated functions on types using `impl` blocks and uses method syntax (`value.method()`) appropriately
- Uses visibility modifiers (`pub`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`) to control access to types, functions, and modules and to design simple public APIs
- Understands and uses basic constant and static declarations (`const`, `static`) without unsafe interior mutation
- Reads and understands compiler error messages and warnings, and iteratively fixes them without excessive trial-and-error","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Ownership, Borrowing, and Lifetimes","scope_included":"- Has a clear mental model of stack vs heap allocation and how ownership controls memory management in Rust
- Applies move semantics correctly, understanding when values are moved, copied, or borrowed, and how this affects subsequent use
- Distinguishes between `Copy` and non-`Copy` types and leverages `Copy` semantics for simple scalar and small types where appropriate
- Uses references (`&T`) and mutable references (`&mut T`) to avoid unnecessary moves and clones while respecting borrowing rules
- Applies the aliasing rules: multiple immutable references allowed, at most one mutable reference at a time, and no simultaneous mutable and immutable references
- Works confidently with common reference-based types: string slices (`&str`), array and slice references (`&[T]`, `&mut [T]`), and references to structs and enums
- Resolves typical borrow checker issues by restructuring code, adjusting scopes, or changing ownership and borrowing patterns instead of forcing clones blindly
- Understands lifetime concepts at a conceptual level (who owns what, how long references must live) and how Rust uses them to prevent dangling references
- Uses lifetime elision rules effectively in function signatures and methods without needing explicit annotations for simple cases
- Can add straightforward lifetime parameters to functions and `impl` blocks when interacting with references in simple data structures or helper utilities
- Avoids `unsafe` code for memory management or lifetimes, and recognizes that advanced lifetime manipulations and unsafe code are beyond their current scope","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Types, Traits, and Generics","scope_included":"- Designs and uses custom data types (`struct`, `enum`) to model domain logic, including basic invariants encoded in type structure
- Implements inherent methods (`impl TypeName { ... }`) to encapsulate behavior on data types, including constructors and utility methods
- Uses the standard trait system to derive common behaviors via `#[derive]` for traits like `Debug`, `Clone`, `Copy`, `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `Hash`, `Default`, and `Ord` where appropriate
- Understands traits as contracts defining shared behavior and uses trait bounds to write reusable functions for operations like comparison or formatting
- Writes generic functions and types that use type parameters (`fn f<T>(...)`, `struct Wrapper<T> { ... }`) for basic reuse across multiple concrete types
- Applies trait bounds (`T: Trait`, `where T: Trait`) in simple cases to constrain generics to types that support required methods or behaviors
- Uses core standard traits in practice, including `From`, `Into`, `AsRef`, `AsMut`, and iterator-related traits at a basic level
- Works with trait objects (`Box<dyn Trait>`, `&dyn Trait`) when dynamic dispatch is acceptable and static generics would overcomplicate the design
- Uses the type inference capabilities of the compiler effectively, supplying explicit type annotations primarily when code clarity or compiler errors require it
- Understands and correctly uses core generic types in the standard library, such as `Option<T>`, `Result<T, E>`, and `Box<T>`, and composes them in function signatures
- Distinguishes between owned data (`String`, `Vec<T>`) and borrowed views (`&str`, `&[T]`) and models APIs with the least restrictive type that satisfies the requirement","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Error Handling and Result Management","scope_included":"- Distinguishes clearly between recoverable errors (modeled with `Result`) and unrecoverable conditions (leading to `panic!`), and chooses the appropriate mechanism
- Uses `Result<T, E>` systematically for operations that can fail (file I/O, parsing, network, conversions) in function signatures and implementations
- Propagates errors in a controlled way using the `?` operator and understands how it desugars to `match` on `Result` or `Option`
- Defines simple error enums and structs to represent domain-specific error conditions and implements basic traits like `Debug` and `Display` for them
- Converts between different error types in basic situations, using `From` implementations or helper methods (`map_err`, `or_else`) where needed
- Uses `Option<T>` for absence of values where failure is not an error condition and converts between `Option` and `Result` when appropriate
- Writes calling code that inspects and responds to errors through pattern matching on `Result`, including retries and fallback behavior in simple workflows
- Avoids overuse of `unwrap`, `expect`, and `panic!` in production-like paths and confines them to cases where failure truly indicates a programming error
- Understands and uses utility methods on `Result` and `Option` (`map`, `and_then`, `unwrap_or`, `ok_or`) to express error-aware logic more succinctly
- Reads error chains and messages from standard and third-party libraries and uses them to diagnose configuration or environment issues","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Collections, Strings, and Iterators","scope_included":"- Chooses appropriate standard collections (`Vec<T>`, `VecDeque<T>`, `HashMap<K, V>`, `HashSet<T>`, `BTreeMap<K, V>`) for storing and organizing data based on access and ordering requirements
- Understands ownership and borrowing implications when inserting into or removing from collections, and avoids common pitfalls involving moved values
- Uses slices (`&[T]`, `&mut [T]`) to pass views into collections without copying, and understands when to return or accept slices in APIs
- Works confidently with Rust’s string model, including `String`, `&str`, and UTF-8 encoding, and understands that indices are byte-based rather than character-based
- Performs basic string operations (concatenation, splitting, searching, trimming, formatting) with attention to allocation overhead and borrowing where possible
- Uses the iterator trait & APIs fluently: converting collections to iterators (`iter`, `iter_mut`, `into_iter`) and consuming results
- Composes iterator chains using adapters (`map`, `filter`, `take`, `skip`, `enumerate`, `flatten`, `zip`, `fold`, `collect`) to transform and aggregate data without unnecessary intermediate allocations
- Understands the difference between consuming and non-consuming iterator adapters and avoids use-after-move mistakes with iterators
- Collects iterators into various targets (`Vec<T>`, `HashMap<K, V>`, `String`, other collection types) with `collect` and type annotations where required
- Uses borrowing correctly within iterator closures, ensuring captured variables obey ownership and lifetime constraints","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Concurrency and Parallelism Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understands Rust’s concurrency safety model and how the type system prevents data races at compile time
- Spawns and manages OS threads using `std::thread::spawn`, passing data to and from threads through moves and result joins
- Uses thread-safe wrappers like `Arc<T>` to share read-only or coordinated mutable data between threads while respecting ownership rules
- Applies synchronization primitives from the standard library (`Mutex<T>`, `RwLock<T>`, `Condvar` in simple cases) to coordinate access to shared mutable state
- Recognizes that `Arc<Mutex<T>>` is a common pattern for shared mutable state and uses it correctly in simple multi-threaded programs
- Communicates between threads using channels (`std::sync::mpsc`) to implement producer–consumer patterns
- Understands `Send` and `Sync` traits at a conceptual level (types safe to move to another thread vs shared between threads) even if not implementing them manually
- Avoids obvious deadlocks and livelocks by following simple locking patterns and minimizing lock hold times in basic concurrent designs
- Distinguishes between concurrency (interleaving tasks) and parallelism (executing in parallel) and knows when threads are appropriate vs async or single-threaded designs
- Uses high-level parallelism helpers (for instance, a data-parallel iterator library) in straightforward use cases when available in the project’s ecosystem","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Modules, Crates, and Project Structure","scope_included":"- Understands the concept of a crate as the compilation and packaging unit in Rust and distinguishes between binary and library crates
- Organizes code into modules and submodules using `mod` declarations and separate files, keeping related functionality together for clarity and reuse
- Manages visibility across modules with `pub` and `pub(crate)` and designs simple, coherent public APIs for internal modules and library crates
- Uses `use` statements and path syntax (absolute and relative) to import types, traits, and functions from other modules and crates without creating cycles
- Creates and maintains multiple binaries or libraries within a single package using Cargo conventions (`src/main.rs`, `src/lib.rs`, `src/bin/`) as needed
- Understands and uses workspaces at a basic level to group related crates under a single repository, sharing configuration and dependencies between them
- Structures simple multi-crate codebases so that shared logic lives in library crates, while binary crates focus on orchestration and I/O concerns
- Reads and updates `Cargo.toml` to manage crate metadata, dependencies, features, and basic build configuration in alignment with project needs","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Async Rust Basics","scope_included":"- Understands the conceptual difference between synchronous and asynchronous I/O and when async is beneficial (high-latency network or disk-bound workloads)
- Writes basic `async fn` functions and uses `.await` to suspend and resume execution at asynchronous operations
- Uses one established async runtime (typically Tokio or an equivalent chosen by the team) to execute async tasks and timers and to perform network I/O
- Uses futures returned by async functions without attempting to implement custom `Future` types or low-level executors
- Combines async tasks using simple patterns (spawning tasks, joining tasks, timeouts) as provided by the chosen runtime
- Handles errors in async code with `Result` and `?`, and understands that async functions return futures that encapsulate both value and error
- Understands Send-related constraints in async contexts at a basic level and follows library guidelines when passing data across tasks or threads
- Avoids blocking calls within async contexts and uses async-compatible APIs for I/O and short compute-bound work where appropriate
- Reads and uses documentation of async ecosystem crates (HTTP clients, database clients) to perform straightforward operations within an async program","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Testing and Code Quality Practices","scope_included":"- Writes unit tests for functions and modules using the standard test framework with `#[test]` annotations
- Groups tests logically within the same file or in `tests/` for integration tests, following Cargo conventions
- Uses assertions (`assert!`, `assert_eq!`, `assert_ne!`, and related macros) to verify correctness of functionality and invariants
- Runs tests regularly using `cargo test` and interprets test output and failures to diagnose broken assumptions or regressions
- Designs functions and modules to be testable by separating pure logic from side effects like I/O or global state
- Uses conditional compilation for tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) to avoid including test-only code in production builds
- Applies basic refactoring to improve code readability and maintainability without changing observable behavior, relying on tests to ensure correctness
- Uses standard tooling to maintain formatting and code quality (rustfmt and Clippy) and treats warnings and lints as actionable feedback for improvement
- Adds simple documentation tests in doc comments to ensure examples compile and stay in sync with the code behavior","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Performance and Memory Awareness","scope_included":"- Understands Rust’s philosophy of zero-cost abstractions and writes code that leverages high-level APIs without sacrificing performance unnecessarily
- Recognizes common sources of unnecessary allocation or copying (creating new `String` or `Vec` instead of borrowing, cloning large structures) and avoids them in simple cases
- Chooses between owned and borrowed data in function interfaces to minimize allocations while still keeping usage ergonomic
- Uses collection operations that align with expected complexity, understanding big-O behavior of common collections for insertion, lookup, and iteration
- Avoids premature micro-optimizations while still being aware of hot paths and high-frequency operations that may warrant closer attention
- Understands that iterators and slices can be as efficient as manual loops and uses them with confidence in performance-sensitive sections where applicable
- Builds release binaries (`cargo build --release`) for performance testing and deployment and knows that debug builds may show very different performance characteristics
- Reads memory- and performance-related compiler warnings and adjusts code where simple fixes increase efficiency or reduce allocations without harming clarity","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Rust Ecosystem and Library Usage","scope_included":"- Uses Cargo to add, update, and remove dependencies and manages version requirements and semantic versioning constraints in `Cargo.toml`
- Evaluates third-party crates at a basic level using metadata (downloads, maintenance, license, documentation quality) to decide whether to adopt them
- Uses serialization and deserialization libraries (for example, a Serde-based stack) to work with JSON, YAML, or other common data formats
- Uses common utility crates for HTTP, logging, randomness, time and date handling, and command-line argument parsing where project requirements demand them
- Understands the concept of feature flags for dependencies and enables or disables features to tailor functionality and binary size
- Reads and applies crate-level configuration and usage patterns from documentation (docs.rs or project README) to integrate external libraries correctly
- Adapts to project-specific ecosystem choices (logging framework, async runtime, testing helpers) and uses them in line with existing code and conventions
- Keeps awareness that cryptographic, unsafe, or low-level FFI crates require more caution and usually additional review by more experienced Rust developers","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Build System, and Environment Setup","scope_included":"- Installs and manages Rust toolchains using `rustup`, including selecting stable, beta, or nightly channels as directed by project requirements
- Uses Cargo as the primary build, run, and package tool: `cargo build`, `cargo run`, `cargo test`, `cargo doc`, and related subcommands
- Configures basic Cargo settings (profiles, simple feature flags, default-run, build dependencies) through `Cargo.toml` and `Cargo.lock`
- Uses IDE or editor support (such as rust-analyzer or an equivalent plugin) for code completion, inline type hints, go-to-definition, and on-the-fly error checking
- Manages toolchain components like `rustfmt`, Clippy, and Rust source installation and keeps local tooling reasonably up to date
- Understands the difference between debug and release builds and switches profiles appropriately for development, testing, and benchmarking
- Builds and runs tests and binaries across multiple targets if the project requires simple cross-compilation setups provided by the team
- Uses `cargo update` and dependency auditing tools (when available in the project) to keep dependencies within expected version ranges and address known vulnerabilities","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Debugging, Profiling, and Observability","scope_included":"- Reads and interprets Rust compiler error messages and warnings to identify type mismatches, borrowing issues, and lifetime problems and resolve them methodically
- Uses runtime output (`println!`, logging macros from the project’s logging framework) to trace execution flow and inspect variable values in simple debugging scenarios
- Enables and inspects backtraces on panics to identify locations of failures and trace through call stacks when diagnosing severe issues
- Uses a debugger (CLI or IDE-integrated, built on gdb or lldb) at a basic level to set breakpoints, step through code, and inspect variables in straightforward cases
- Adds and configures basic logging within Rust applications using a standard logging facade and implementation, ensuring logs include levels and simple context
- Uses timing measurements and simple benchmarking approaches provided by the team to compare performance of alternative implementations in local development
- Collects and analyzes error logs and simple metrics from running services or binaries (when infrastructure is present) to understand failure modes and load patterns
- Collaborates with more experienced team members to interpret advanced profiling results and apply targeted optimizations suggested by those results","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation, and Code Review","scope_included":"- Documents public APIs and key modules using Rust doc comments (`///`) and module-level documentation, explaining purpose, behavior, and usage expectations
- Writes clear examples in documentation that can be run as doc tests, keeping them in sync with real code behavior and ensuring they compile
- Follows the team’s Rust style and coding conventions, including naming, module organization, error handling patterns, and logging approaches
- Participates in code reviews by explaining design choices, asking clarifying questions, and responding constructively to feedback from more experienced Rust developers
- Reviews peers’ Rust code for basic correctness, clarity, and adherence to established patterns, even if not yet able to critique advanced lifetime or unsafe usage
- Communicates limitations in Rust expertise clearly and seeks guidance for complex patterns (advanced lifetimes, unsafe code, FFI, complex async coordination)
- Uses version control workflows (branching, pull requests, commit messages) in a way that makes Rust changes easy to review and track
- Contributes to and maintains project-level documentation (README, architecture notes, runbooks) documenting how Rust components fit into the broader system","is_non_core_section":true}]}
